coredata.dc:title: Bioactive and trackable MXene quantum dots for subcellular nanomedicine applications
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2020 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Rafieerad, Alireza
Yan, Weiang
Amiri, Ahmad
Dhingra, Sanjiv
coredata.dc:description: Next-generation “early transition metal carbides and nitrides” (MXene) materials have facile and economic processing with simultaneous synthesis and functionalization. Their surface characteristics can be tailored for specific applications, including energy storage, sensors, and water filtration, by adjusting synthetic parameters. Furthermore, MXene possesses excellent biocompatibility and can be designed for anticancer, antibacterial, and immunomodulatory properties. However, a major limitation facing systemic delivery of nanoparticles is ensuring successful uptake from the bloodstream into the desired site of action. An important consideration is the vascular endothelial cell (EC), which serves as the barrier between blood and organs. Therefore, cellular uptake of engineered nanoparticles by EC is important to advance their use in nanomedicine. In this study, smart zero-dimensional biocompatible titanium carbide (Ti3C2Tx) MXene quantum dots (MQD) were synthesized with specific surface modifications for subcellular nanomedicine applications. MQD were shown to be spontaneously uptaken into human ECs within 24 h of cell culture. MQD localization was observed to be highly stable during this with no significant changes seen in cell morphology. Furthermore, uptaken MQD maintained their strong autofluorescence properties at different excitation-emission wavelengths to enable post-delivery monitoring and tracking. This novel finding represents promising progress in the application of MQD for nanomedicine.
coredata.dcterms:subject: MXene quantum dots
Bioactive surfaces
Hydrothermal process
Cellular uptake
Endothelial cells

head.ce:title: Bioactive and trackable MXene quantum dots for subcellular nanomedicine applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Regenerative Medicine Program, Institute of Cardiovascular Sciences, St. Boniface Hospital Research Centre, Department of Physiology and Pathophysiology, Rady Faculty of Health Sciences, University of Manitoba, Winnipeg, Canada

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Regenerative Medicine Program, Institute of Cardiovascular Sciences, St. Boniface Hospital Research Centre, Department of Physiology and Pathophysiology, Rady Faculty of Health Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Regenerative Medicine Program, Institute of Cardiovascular Sciences, St. Boniface Hospital Research Centre, Department of Physiology and Pathophysiology, Rady Faculty of Health Sciences, University of Manitoba, Winnipeg, Canada

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Texas A&M University, College Station, TX 77843, United States

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Texas A&M University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Texas A&M University, College Station, Texas 77843, United States

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Regenerative Medicine Program, Director: Canada Italy Tissue Engineering Program, Institute of Cardiovascular Sciences, St. Boniface Hospital Research Centre, R-3028-2, 351 Tache Avenue, Winnipeg, R2H2A6, Canada.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: AR, WY, and AA contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Next-generation “early transition metal carbides and nitrides” (MXene) materials have facile and economic processing with simultaneous synthesis and functionalization. Their surface characteristics can be tailored for specific applications, including energy storage, sensors, and water filtration, by adjusting synthetic parameters. Furthermore, MXene possesses excellent biocompatibility and can be designed for anticancer, antibacterial, and immunomodulatory properties. However, a major limitation facing systemic delivery of nanoparticles is ensuring successful uptake from the bloodstream into the desired site of action. An important consideration is the vascular endothelial cell (EC), which serves as the barrier between blood and organs. Therefore, cellular uptake of engineered nanoparticles by EC is important to advance their use in nanomedicine. In this study, smart zero-dimensional biocompatible titanium carbide (Ti3C2Tx) MXene quantum dots (MQD) were synthesized with specific surface modifications for subcellular nanomedicine applications. MQD were shown to be spontaneously uptaken into human ECs within 24 h of cell culture. MQD localization was observed to be highly stable during this with no significant changes seen in cell morphology. Furthermore, uptaken MQD maintained their strong autofluorescence properties at different excitation-emission wavelengths to enable post-delivery monitoring and tracking. This novel finding represents promising progress in the application of MQD for nanomedicine.

ce:sections.ce:para: The design of functional ultra-small carbon-based materials to target biological systems is a rapidly emerging research area with important implications for biomedical applications. In particular, the ability to tailor materials for specific and dynamic interactions with human cells and tissues offers significant opportunities for the application of materials-based science and technology. To this end, various composites and surface modifications have been developed to refine cell-material interactions and to enhance material performance for tissue engineering. These efforts have led to significant progress in optimizing the biocompatibility, long-term safety, and mechanistic bioactivity of zero, one, and two-dimensional nanostructured carbon materials (NCM).

Within the last decade, modified nanoscale graphene-based materials have been designed for antibacterial, antiviral, and anticancer functions. However, clinical translation of this technology has been limited by several gaps in knowledge, including potential toxicity, unclear biological mechanism, and poor integration of nanomaterials into living biologic systems. In particular, graphene materials are not readily uptaken by the endothelium for transit from bloodstream into target tissues [1]. This represents a major limitation to their operation in nanomedicine. Recent advances in nanoparticle technology have resulted in the development of stable homogeneous colloidal NCM suspensions, such as graphene quantum dots (GQD), that may address some of these limitations [2]. These zero-dimensional (0D) materials were designed to have superior biocompatibility and improved biological functionality over conventional nanomaterials [3]; functional carbon dots have been engineered for therapeutics through direct material-cell interactions, delivery of conjugated drugs and growth-factors, or bioassembly of cell-loaded scaffolds. Additional autofluorescent or ferromagnetic properties have also been incorporated into quantum dot technology for in vivo monitoring and tracking of delivered nanoparticles. Therefore, application of these quantum dots holds significant therapeutic promise for regenerative medicine.

More recently, a new generation of the NCM named “early transition-metal carbide and nitride” (MXene) have been synthesized [4]. These porous metal-carbide structures retain the biocompatibility of previous generation materials but can be engineered to enhance biofunctionality [5–7]. For example, titanium carbide (Ti3C2Tx) MXene quantum dots (MQD) have been synthesized to possess novel intrinsic immunomodulatory properties not previously seen with graphene materials [5]. The next major barrier to translation is ensuring successful transit of these nanoparticles from the bloodstream through the endothelium and into the desired site of action [8–10].

In this study, a targeted fabrication process for highly bioactive 0D Ti3C2Tx MQD is described. Surface bioactivity was maximized through controlled exfoliation of MAX phase substrate to obtain the optimum density of active functional groups for MXene end-products. These surface functional groups provide excellent optical properties and facilitate spontaneous and efficient internalization of MQD into human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVEC) without any uptake enhancing techniques. These findings represent promising progress in the design and application of autofluorescent MQD for nanomedicine.

Accordion-like two-dimensional Ti3C2Tx MXene nanosheets (MNS) were formed through acid etching of the bulky Ti3AlC2 MAX phase material (Schematic view: Graphical Abstract A). Subsequently, highly bioactive and trackable MQD with an average diameter of 4.58 nm were synthesized from Ti3C2Tx MNS using a facile, straightforward, and environmentally-friendly hydrothermal process. The main portion of the surface functional groups of MQD formed during the initial exfoliation of MNS is further enhanced by hydrothermal reaction. The surface characteristics and exposed functional groups of MQD can be tailored for specific applications by adjusting the hydrothermal settings; these changes fine-tune the varying impacts of polarity, oxidation, and solvents or dispersion mediums to alter the surface architecture of the final product. As-prepared MQD have large surface area with highly stable aqueous dispersibility and an abundance of –F, –O, –OH, –COOH, and –NH functional groups to facilitate interaction with human cells and tissues.

Successful synthesis of MQD was confirmed using transmission electron microscopy (FEI Talos F200X S/TEM, Thermo Fisher) and energy dispersive X-Ray spectroscopy (EDS, Nova NanoSEM 450). High resolution TEM images revealed the homogeneous crystalline morphology of MQD with similar particle dimensions and lattice d-spacing of 2.15 Å associating to the (0110) facets of Ti3C2Tx crystal. EDS mapping images illustrated the well-defined distribution of the carbon, oxygen and titanium elements in the composition of MQD (Graphical Abstract B). FT-IR analysis (Thermo Nicolet Nexus 870, Thermo Fisher Scientific) was used to verify the chemical structure and composition of MQD. In the FT-IR spectrum of aqueous Ti3C2Tx MQD, dominant peaks at around 3400, 3100, 1650, 1450, 1000, and 660 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of –OH, –NH, C–O/C=O, OH, CF and Ti–C/Ti–F/Ti–O function groups respectively (Graphical Abstract C).

Next, the absorbance and fluorescence properties of the MQD were evaluated to determine the tracking potential of MQD. To this end, UV–Vis spectroscopy and fluorescence measurements of colloidal MXene nanomaterial were conducted at different wavelengths. The absorbance spectrum of MQD showed a dominant peak at a wavelength of around 300 nm with a linear concentration-absorbance profile. This peak corresponds to the nanocarbon bonds in the structure of Ti3C2Tx. Strong optical absorption performance is also seen at additional wavelengths attributable to functional groups available on the surface of colloidal MQD suspensions and subtracted Ti3C2Tx crystal dots. This unique absorption signature of MQD can be leveraged for dose tracking and monitoring during therapy with an appropriate wavelength chosen based on the specific application (Supplementary Fig. 1A).

MQD also displayed significant autofluorescence properties at different wavelengths (Supplementary Fig. 1BI). Key excitation-emission peaks were seen at wavelengths of 395, 490, and 660 nm, corresponding to the commonly used DAPI (excitation 377 nm/emission 447 nm), GFP (excitation 469 nm/emission 525 nm), RFP (excitation 555 nm/emission 584 nm), Texas Red (excitation 586 nm/emission 647 nm), and Cy5 (excitation 628 nm/emission 685 nm) filters for fluorescent cellular imaging (Merged Image in Graphical Abstract D, Individual Filters in Supplementary Fig. 2). Pristine autofluorescent MQD could be easily visualized with strong fluorescent signals at all these wavelengths when monitored using a fluorescence microscope (Cytation 5 Cell Imaging Multi-Mode Reader, BioTek Instruments).

Next, the clinical translatability of smart Ti3C2Tx MQD was studied using HUVEC. Homogenous colloidal suspensions of aqueous MQD at two different concentrations (2 and 20 μgml−1) were co-cultured with the HUVEC for 24 h to study the material-cell interactions. Cells were then fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde, mounted using Prolong™ Diamond Antifade reagent with DAPI (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA), and visualized by high resolution fluorescence microscopy (Nikon Eclipse Ti-2). Autofluorescent MQD were clearly internalized into the live HUVEC after 24 h of incubation in a dose-dependent fashion (Graphical Abstract E and Supplementary Fig. 3). MQD can be readily seen in bright-field and its fluorescence was predominantly visualized at GFP and RFP wavelengths (with DAPI showing the cell nuclei in blue). Internalized Ti3C2Tx nanoparticles were confined to the cytoplasm of the cells and did not significantly influence cellular morphology and behaviour during the study period.

In summary, this study demonstrated the design and synthesis of tailored titanium carbide MXene quantum dots using a targeted and facile hydrothermal process. MQD have an enhanced active surface area when compared with planar MXene nanosheets with an abundance of–OH, –COOH and –NH functional groups. Furthermore, this study demonstrated that bioactive and autofluorescent MQD are readily uptaken into human endothelial cells. This important finding provides mechanistic rationale for the therapeutic efficacy seen in previous studies of MQD. The endothelium serves as the predominant barrier between blood vessels and tissues and MQD delivered intravenously must successfully traverse this barrier to reach their targets. The optical properties of MQD also enable effective dose monitoring and post-delivery tracking. This sets the groundwork for further experiments evaluating these properties in vivo and brings us closer to clinical translation of this technology.

ce:section.ce:para: This work was supported by a grant from Canadian Institutes of Health Research (# PJT156148) and CANUSA funding to Dr. Sanjiv Dhingra. W.Y. is supported by a CIHR Fellowship (MFE-171305).
The authors declare no competing interests.

Alireza Rafieerad: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal Analysis, Validation, Visualization, Writing – Original Draft, Writing – Review & Editing. Weiang Yan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal Analysis, Validation, Visualization, Writing – Original Draft, Writing – Review & Editing. Ahmad Amiri: Investigation, Validation, Visualization, Writing – Original Draft, Writing – Review & Editing. Sanjiv Dhingra: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal Analysis, Supervision, Resources, Writing – Original Draft, Writing – Review & Editing.
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matdes.2020.109091.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The role of the lateral dimension of graphene oxide in the regulation of cellular responses
Biomaterials.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Yue, W. Wei, Z. Yue, B. Wang, N. Luo, Y. Gao, D. Ma, G. Ma, Z. Su, The role of the lateral dimension of graphene oxide in the regulation of cellular responses, Biomaterials. 33 (2012) 4013-4021. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biomaterials.2012.02.021.
